summary: This chapter from Tom Mitchell's Machine Learning textbook introduces joint probability distributions and how they enable probabilistic inference for machine learning tasks. It explains how any conditional or marginal probability can be computed from a joint distribution, and discusses the data scarcity problem when variables are numerous. It then presents two foundational probability estimation methods: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation.
bullets: Joint probability distributions over random variables let you answer any probabilistic query by summing relevant table rows.
With n boolean variables the table has 2^n rows, making reliable estimation infeasible for high-dimensional data.
MLE estimates P(X=1) as the simple fraction α1/(α1+α0) of observed outcomes.
MAP extends MLE by adding γ1 and γ0 imaginary heads/tails to encode prior beliefs and confidence.
MLE is a special case of MAP (γ1=γ0=0), and prior influence vanishes as observed data grows.
